messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: Nonlinear Structural Equation Modeling in R
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: Nonlinear Structural Equation Modeling in R
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Enhance your statistical toolkit with advanced modeling techniques for complex observational data. Nonlinear Structural Equation Modeling is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 11 with Holmes Finch (Department of Educational Psychology - Ball State University). Epidemiological researchers can apply these advanced methods to model complex, non-additive exposure-outcome pathways and latent health states more accurately. By integrating nonlinear structural equation modeling into your R workflow, you can better estimate threshold effects, latent interactions, and curvilinear growth trajectories in longitudinal cohort data. This allows for more robust causal mediation analyses and realistic measurement models when analyzing observational health datasets.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/nonlinear-structural-equation-modeling) to reserve your place before it starts, and feel free to circulate this with colleagues and students!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Overview of the GDIS Hub
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Enhance your epidemiological research by integrating robust, disaggregated statistical data into your analytical workflows. Overview of the GDIS Hub (Free Seminar) is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 23, led by Fran?ois Marshall (Statistics Canada). This upcoming seminar introduces the Gender, Diversity and Inclusion Statistics (GDIS) Hub as a major entry point for disaggregated and intersectional statistical evidence. Epidemiologists can leverage these robust, high-quality public data resources to move beyond aggregate summaries and illuminate health heterogeneity across intersecting social categories. By mastering the Hub's structure and data holdings, you can integrate official statistics directly into your R-based epidemiological models to improve the precision of your empirical claims and strengthen peer-reviewed publications.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/overview-of-the-gdis-hub-free-seminar) to secure your spot, and please share this opportunity with colleagues and students who might benefit!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Last chance: AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis
messages.subject_clean: Last chance: AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis
messages.body_plain:
Hello R-SIG-EPI members

Last chance - Synthesizing vast bodies of evidence efficiently is becoming increasingly critical for modern scientific research. AI-Assisted Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis is a 2-day seminar livestreaming June 10 and 12 with Moses Boudourides (Data Science Graduate Program, School of Professional Studies, Northwestern University). Epidemiologists can leverage these AI-assisted workflows to rapidly screen and synthesize massive volumes of clinical and observational study literature. By integrating open bibliographic APIs and machine learning models, you can streamline the screening and data extraction phases of your meta-analyses while maintaining the strict transparency required for public health publications. This allows your research team to focus on interpreting epidemiological patterns and running robust statistical analyses in R rather than getting bogged down in manual database searches.

Sign up today (https://instats.org/seminar/ai-assisted-systematic-reviews-and-meta) to join before enrollment closes, and please pass this to colleagues and students who may be interested!


Best wishes

Michael Zyphur
Professor and Director
Instats | instats.org
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] BART models for species distribution modelling (SDMs)
messages.subject_clean: BART models for species distribution modelling (SDMs)
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,
 
we are pleased to announce our online course: Species Distribution Modelling with Bayesian Additive Regression Tree (BART) methods
 

Dates: 1?3 July - Daily schedule: 15:00?18:00 (Berlin time)


 
 
 
Course website: https://www.physalia-courses.org/courses-workshops/barts/
 
 
 
 
 
This course introduces BART models for species distribution modelling (SDMs) and shows how they can improve prediction and interpretation compared to traditional approaches.
 
Using R and the embarcadero and dbarts packages, participants will learn the full SDM workflow: from predictor selection and model training to evaluation, interpretation, and prediction in new environments: 
 
Introduction to SDMs and BART theory
Hands-on modelling in R (embarcadero, dbarts)
Predictor selection, model fitting, and evaluation
Interpretation of effects and spatial predictions
Advanced topics including random-intercept BARTs
 
 
 
We look forward to welcoming you!
 
Best regards,
 
 
 
Carlo
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
--------------------

Carlo Pecoraro, Ph.D


Physalia-courses DIRECTOR

info at physalia-courses.org

mobile: +49 17645230846

[ Bluesky ]( https://bsky.app/profile/physaliacourses.bsky.social ) [ Linkedin ]( https://www.linkedin.com/in/physalia-courses-a64418127/ )
 
	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]
